And I oppose the employment relations bill in its entirety.
I'd like to talk to you about
what I've seen in my working life as a PSA delegate,
the impact of the 90 day rule on employees, both in terms
of feeling insecure in their jobs
and fear of changing jobs
as they might be unemployed after 90 days.
It was very evident from 2013
that removing the 30 days in which an employment contract
can be reviewed against a collective employment agreement,
left new employees very much in the dark about their
employment agreement rights, the introduction of a new class
of worker specified contractors who have no holiday pay,
sick leave, maternity leave, parental leave,
family violence, leave to name a few, no retirement savings,
no minimum wage criteria specified,
and will themselves be responsible for a CC levies
and other criteria could open the doors to exploitation,
particularly of those in contracted casual work
and those with multiple contract jobs
and drive wages to the bottom and tendering for contracts.
In addition to this, the bill introduces higher
to fire tactics for those earning over 180,000 per annum,
which is very low, uh, boundary
and scales back personal grievance options
for all other workers by removing any remedy
for personal grievance.
If the employer was determined to have contributed
to the situation,
this weights the game entirely in favor of the employer.
If this bill is passed, it will be legal for employee
to poorly treat employees options for a fair
and reasonable solution
by good faith bargaining are off the table.
The justification for this bill is
to support greater market flexibility
and going for growth
to deliver higher productivity and wages.
This bill will only deliver reduced wages,
lower opportunities and conditions,
and bring unnecessary hardship
and stress to workers, including the loss
of access to justice.
Just as women workers have been undercut
with the equal pay employment, equal pay amendment bill
and 12.8 billion has been withdrawn from the economy in
terms of wages forfeited.
So a further downgrading of future investment
and spending by workers will be disabled by this bill.
Workers won't be able to pay taxes spend
and investors they could have, which reduces the tax
for the take for the government, the Bill of roads,
workers' rights, and will reduce their future earning
prospects, causing a spiraling downward system leading
to a poorer economic outcome for New Zealand.
This bill permits a tremendous degree of power on the,
on the part of the employer
to impose specified contractor status on employees which
could lead, which will lead to forced misclassification.
Overall, the bill will lead to
unaddressed poor working conditions
and exploitation, which will in turn impact on productivity.
The bill is going in the wrong direction,
undoing the fair labor laws that New Zealand is founded on
passing of this bill will inflict harm due to the asymmetry
of power between the employer and the employee.
It won't reduce red tape
and compliance as a contracting arrangements proposed are in
fact, more complex
and sacrificing workers' rights will not lead to growth.
To conclude, I recommend the Select Committee abandon
abandon this bill in entirely
and go back to the drawing board, defining the issue,
consulting more widely to address all employment
and business concerns while respecting the very important
process of unions and protecting worker rights.
Thank you.
Thanks very much, Gail.
Um, we've got time for our question from Glen,
Uh, Kia. Gail, thanks
for your written and oral submission.
It's, um, powerful.
Um, in your written submission, you talk about living
through the Employment Contracts Act era.
Um, are there lessons you can take from that time
to this piece of legislation
that you wanna share with us, please?
Well, yes. Thank you for the opportunity to share that.
At the time I was a single income, no for a family of four,
two children and one husband.
And, um, uh, I was, it was just so difficult.
Unemployment was high. I was a woman,
so I'm in a disadvantage in the first place.
I actually lost one job because I was a woman
and not considered, um, you know, um,
of worthy status to be maintained.
And, um, and the pressure to just
stay alive and working
and receiving an income to be able
to support your family was awful.
And that was with the,
and then later in 2030 13 was the employment, um,
contracts, uh, sorry, the readmission of the 30 day thing.
I saw many, um, employees, um, you know,
losing out on rights
and opportunities they would otherwise have had.
So that's, that's it from me.
It is very difficult when you are
personally in this situation.
Thanks, uh, very much Gail.
Appreciate the time you've given us and for your submission.
Um, we're gonna move on to our next submitter now.
Thanks a lot. Thank you.
If I can invite the Workers First Union, um,
I think Anita is presenting on their behalf,
if you could turn on your mic, uh,
and turn on your video for us, it'd be appreciated.
Anita, we've got 10 minutes with you this afternoon,
including any time for questions.
Kia, thank you. Over you Kia.
Thanks for having me.
Um, you obviously have our written submission
so you are aware that we are, um,
against this piece of legislation.
Um, my name is Aha Rosen Trader.
I'm the strategic project coordinator
for Workers First Union.
Um, and today I wanted to speak to you, um,
rather than going through sort of
what we've already got in our written submission, I wanted
to speak to you about a few examples of, um, things
that we've sort of dealt with as a union, um,
in the real world that, uh, um,
potentially would've been quite different
if they had occurred under these, um, proposed new laws.
So, to start with, just on the personal grievance
amendments, um, we've got two cases
that I wanna refer to, um, that had particular outcomes
and then talk about how they would've been different under
the new proposals.
The first is, um, Knuth, uh, versus Kmart, which went
to the Employment Relations Authority.
Um, and the second is Harris versus the Warehouse,
which went to the authority
and then to the employment court.
So these are both publicly available.
Um, in the first Knuth versus, um, Kmart, the worker,
Michelle Knuth, was returning
to Kmarts Palmerston North Store where she worked
after a paid break
and saw two security guards being attacked by a shoplifter.
Um, she stepped in to protect the guards, she separated them
and she de escalated the situation.
Um, so the incident was caught on CCTV
and I mean, most people would hear about this
and think, you know, this woman, she's a hero.
Um, she did the right thing.
But unfortunately, what actually happened in the end was, is
that Kmart dismissed Michelle
for allegedly breaching its code of conduct
and what it called its five D's policy, which is headed,
don't be a hero when it comes to your safety.
Under this policy, Michelle, um, was supposed to just
walk away and let the, let the incident continue no matter
what happened to the human beings involved.
Um, thankfully Michelle was able
to raise a personal grievance, um,
and was reinstated to her former role
by the Employment Relations Authority.
Um, if she had raised the personal grievance under these new
propo, these proposed new laws, um,
she would be very unlikely to win reinstatement
if the authority maintained.
It was serious misconduct, which came up labeled it as such,
because obviously the category of the categories
of misconduct and serious misconduct are just
essentially defined by employers,
however they see fit in these circumstances.
Um, if it was found to be serious misconduct conduct,
she wouldn't be entitled to any remedies at all.
Um, and if it was maintained that she contributed, um,
which her employer would, would argue she had
by breaching the policy, she wouldn't be entitled
to reinstatement or compensation.
So it's very likely that at the end of that,
even though she'd absolutely categorically done the right
thing in human terms, that she would lose her job
and also not have any opportunity to, um,
claim for compensation.
The second example, um, Harris versus the Warehouse.
So Margaret Harris was dismissed from her role
as a security guard at the warehouse in Kohi.
A couple entered the shop with a dog, um,
Margaret told them the dog wasn't allowed in a disagreement,
ensued the couple then sent a very racist written complaint
to the company in which they asserted the dog
was cleaner than Margaret.
And Margaret was sacked.
Margaret took a personal grievance to the authority and won,
but her compensation was reduced by a hundred percent due
to contribution.
So she was rude to the customer.
The employment court overturned that thankfully
reinstated Margaret, but still reduced her compensation
So the current system allows for that.
There are consequences already for contribution,
but the proposed new laws would see someone like Margaret
without any available remedies,
what should whatsoever she would be without a job
and without any compensation, all
because of rule breaking customers who had it in for her
because of the color of her skin.
Um, and the last thing I wanted to address, um, is the
proposed, um, amendments to Section six, the definition
of employee, what the average New Zealander thinks
of when they think of workers' rights.
Things like minimum wage, holiday pay, sick leave,
job security, union membership, collective bargaining.
These are all actually employee rights.
If you're not an employee,
you don't have these rights, none of them.
And if you're a genuine contractor, someone in business
of their own accord, you don't actually need them.
You are paying yourself, right?
And so if you need to take time off work, then you know,
you let yourself take time off work.
Um, you take on all the risks and you reap all the rewards.
More and more we have workers
who are being told they're contractors,
but are really employees.
They take on increasing risk,
but are very deliberately locked out of collect,
collecting the rewards.
Uber drivers are quite an egregious example of this.
They shall out for an expensive new car, the fuel,
the insurance, the bulk of the costs of doing business.
Meanwhile, they have no control over the money they make.
Uber decides the fear
and how much the driver is paid from it.
Surprise, surprise, this has equated
to less than the minimum wage.
No matter what a driver does, no matter how well he drives,
how clean or comfortable his car is, no matter
how much he charms the, the passenger, he makes
what Uber decides to pay him.
He's also barred from building up a clientele.
So he has no way of generating goodwill for him himself,
only for the real business here, which is Uber.
Meanwhile, Uber is making billions of dollars
and doing everything it can
to avoid paying taxes here and around the world.
Why would we support such a company
to exploit New Zealanders, use our roads
and other infrastructure to enrich themselves with little
to no benefit for us?
Um, now this idea that people are signing up
to be contractors, so therefore that's what they should be.
Well, most of these workers haven't even read their
so-called contract.
They're impossibly long written in legal gibberish, um,
and tiny text on a teeny smartphone screen
and a big agree button is all that stands in the way
of just getting on with the job.
Companies like Uber don't care
whether you've read the contract.
How can they argue? People are knowingly
and willingly signing, signing up
to whatever arrangement is described within them.
The Uber contract actually says, you are not an employee.
You are not a contractor.
Uh, you're not our business partner.
What it doesn't say is what you actually supposedly are,
which they still haven't been able to articulate.
You know, it could say anything.
The agreement could say you're signing up to be our slave.
And people would still sign it
because they're not, they don't read it,
they're not aware of what's in it.
And as far as they're concerned,
it's a take it or leave it offer.
So they don't have any room to negotiate anyway.
And certainly we wouldn't turn, turn around in
that situation and say, well, too bad you signed it
and therefore you're stuck.
You're stuck as a slave.
Um, and the fact is that these amendments will affect many
more workers than just Uber drivers.
They're being made to the very gateway.
Any worker must pass through to access their rights.
The gig, gig economies,
emergence has effectively put us in a position where we have
to do something, we have to pick a side,
and we have to draw a line.
Unfortunately, these amendments are
going in the wrong direction.
Uh, we need to be protecting workers
and ensuring employers contribute positively to society,
not to as detriment.
Um, these are the main points that I wanted to make, um,
orally in addition to our, our written submission.
So thank you for allowing me to make them
and, um, for giving me your time and attention.
I welcome any questions.
Got time for a quick question. Chana,
Uh, Kiara, Anita, thank you very much
for your very sobering, uh, submission
and thank you for all the work that
Workers Fashion Union does.
Uh, and, and just to acknowledge those two, uh,
examples you gave around remedies being removed, I think
that was very important for the US the committee to hear.
I, I do have a question.
You did talk about these amendments going in the wrong
direction, and from what I'm hearing from submitters today,
everybody, uh, it seems to be going in the wrong direction.
What would it look like if it was
going in the right direction?
Um, I, it's a really good question. Thanks, Tiana.
Um, there's quite a lot of, um, there's quite a lot
of examples of this around the world
because obviously we've all been contending
with the gig economy for the same length of time.
And I guess some people could argue
that New Zealand had a bit of a lag
because we didn't get companies like Uber as quickly as some
of the other countries around the world did.
Um, but it's been long enough that there's been,
been quite a lot of action, um, in,
in regard to these things now.
So we have examples in Europe, for example, there was an,
um, a directive that came from the European Union
to European Union member states,
and it basically just said, um, cut the crap.
These workers are employees all the time, every time.
So you should really just legislate to ensure
that they're recognized as employees no matter what.
Because these companies are so good at ducking
and diving, you know, dodging and weaving, um,
and convincing people
that they're not entitled to their rights.
There's been a number of, um, court cases around the world,
um, that have found in favor of, of, um, gag workers
against their employers, um, particularly against Uber.
And we've, um, won our own ones here as well.
And there's a, a, a case
before the Supreme Court at the moment
that we have a decision for in the coming months.
Um, so I think that, you know,
the courts are doing it for us.
Like we are seeing, um,
we are seeing the line being drawn effectively
for the courts, but if we were going to regulate, um,
we should be doing it to try
and protect workers' rights, not to erode them.
Okay, thanks very much, uh, Anita, that's all.
We've got time for this afternoon.
Appreciate, um, your submission. Thank you.
So we're now moving on to the Coman Workers Council.
Uh, Pamela, if you are, uh, there,
if you could turn your video and your mic on.
Um, and we've got 10 minutes with you this afternoon.
Wow, Kia, thank you so much.
Uh, um, Pamela Greeley
and I'm speaking today on behalf of Carman Workers Council.
We are a community based organization
that stands alongside workers across the Carman Peninsula
and, um, slightly further into the h Iraqi area.
Um, we support rural workers, seasonal workers, fauna,
who are already living with uncertainty
in their jobs and incomes.
Um, and we also, um, we set up the workers council
because we can't get union representation
because so many of our people that come to us for help, uh,
are in insecure jobs.
They are in, um, casual contracts.
They're in short hours contracts.
Um, they don't have a regular work schedule.
This view, this bill in our view is the biggest step
backwards for workers' rights in a generation.
Um, it doesn't stand alone.
It comes on top of other recent changes, compounds a crisis
of job insecurity.
And at its heart, the bill tilts the balance
of power further away from ordinary people
and towards employers stripping away protections
that underpin fairness, dignity, and stability at work.
I want to emphasize secure work is not only good
for workers, it's good for employers, for families,
for communities, and for the economy.
This bill erodes
that security at precisely a time when Teo needs it most.
Um, we want to, you know, there's broader economic
and social implications.
Um, as far as we can see the workforce stability
and productivity.
We need to emphasize that insecure work leads
to high turnover, lower productivity,
and less investment in skills
and training, which hurts not only workers,
but also employers in the economy.
And we wanna stress that rural
and small town communities are disproportionately affected
by insecure work, job instability, accelerates,
depopulation, weakens local economies
and undermines social cohesion.
And there's growing evidence
that links precarious employment with higher rates
of stress, mental health struggles,
and poorer health outcomes,
which creates downstream costs for the health system.
Other OECD countries are strengthening workers' protections
to respond to gig economy risk, whereas we are falling
behind if we move in the opposite direction.
And the bill potentially conflicts
with international Labor organization conventions
that we have ratified as, as LT oa, the right
to collective bargaining protection from unfair Dismissal.
And we're in danger of undermining our, uh,
international credibility.
And while the bill appears to give work, uh,
employers more flexibility in practice,
we believe it fosters churn, it reduces trust,
and it discourages employee loyalty, all
of which are very costly to businesses.
And I run a small business alongside my advocacy.
So I do know that this is really a, a, a strong impact.
Um, and we are creating new, weaker categories
of rights like specified contractor, which we're, you know,
we're inviting more disputes
and legal challenges, which adds complexity for businesses,
which I'm sure is in what this bill is, is aimed to do.
Um, I'm also very concerned about the fact that it's looking
to remove dismissal protections for high earners,
which could silence senior employees who would
otherwise challenge unethical or unlawful practices.
And I was talking to, um, a very high earner this morning
who's having a bit of a conflict,
um, with his current company.
And I mentioned this to him
and he said, what do you mean if I earn over $180,000,
I don't have any remedy to take a personal grievance.
And I said, mark, you know, you, you are earning enough.
Maybe they feel that, you know, you've got enough savings.
I've got no idea about his personal circumstances.
But it's just, you know, again, it's another backward step
that you're kind of only certain people can apply
for this for remedies.
Um, and you know, again, with that, women,
Maori Pacifica migrants,
disabled workers are overrepresented in insecure work.
And I believe that this,
these amendments would widen existing inequalities.
Um, I think we should be, um, looking for an opportunity
to reassert a vision
of work in New Zealand grounded in
dignity, fairness, and balance.
And the bill isn't just a technical bill,
it affects the daily lives of family far now
and whole communities.
So this is really for me, what kind
of society do we want to build?
Do we want workplaces where people feel free to safe
and free to speak up to plan for their futures,
to invest in their communities?
Or do we want a future of churn in security and silence?
So I would urge you to reject the substantive provisions
of this bill and instead move forward with employment law
that strengthens balance, fairness,
and dignity for all, which are principles
that have long defined rah as a fair society.
Thank you.
Thanks so much, uh, for that.
Are there any questions from our members? China?
Uh, k Pamela, thank you.
Thank you very much for your submission
and for, uh, particularly
around your comments on potential chilling,
chilling effects this could have on freedom of expression.
I think that was very interesting to hear.
I I I was wondering, um, uh, around unintended consequences,
the, the sorts of impacts you,
you might see at a community like Cora Mandel.
Um, uh, uh, you were talking earlier around, uh, uh,
the impacts on rural workers,
the rural impacts on provincial workers.
Do you have any examples which the, the committee,
what might be interesting
for the committee in your community in, in the koman, uh,
that we should be aware of in terms of impacts on community,
uh, people moving away, that kind of thing? Potentially.
So we, we are noticing there's a big shift at the moment
in much larger companies, um, creating redundancy pools
for people who, who are already in short, short hours works.
Um, and what's happening then is that people are looking at,
they're having to move, they are having to move away.
So they're moving to larger cities
where there's more opportunities for them, which means
that our economy, we've lost a number
of businesses outta the eco um, economy recently.
If you look in Thas, um, which is like one of the big, um,
gateway to the Corman, I think it's is how it,
we've got 19 empty businesses, um,
because they've got no workers.
People aren't there to work for them.
They, they've all gone elsewhere.
They're moving up to Auckland, they're off to Hamilton
because they perceive that there's more opportunity
for them, which in turn means
that what's happening is we are losing our schools
because we can't get the roles there are diminishing
'cause people are moving away
to get their job, um, more jobs.
Um, and then we lose all our services.
We are losing our social services,
our hospitals are suffering.
We can't get teachers, we can't get, this is a real,
a real challenge for rural communities, um, to be able
to stay cohesive because everything then suffers.
And we do see that people are frightened.
They come to us asking us, this is not right.
How do we address it given
all these things that are happening?
Because I don't wanna lose my job and have to move.
Okay. Um, thank you for, um, that answer.
Uh, that brings our hearing to a close
and brings our hearings for, uh, the day to a close members.
So we're now gonna move to the private link. Thank you.

Monday 25 August 2025 | Education and Workforce Subcommittee A

Employment Relations Amendment Bill

Dan Mills

Te Runanga o Nga Toa Awhina, NZPSA
